title: Quiz: Topological Quantum Information Theory
description: Test your understanding of anyons, topological codes, and fault-tolerant quantum computation

questions.question: What distinguishes anyons from bosons and fermions?

options.text: Anyons have mass while bosons and fermions are massless
Anyons acquire a phase factor other than 1 or -1 when exchanged
Anyons can only exist in three dimensions
Anyons cannot be entangled

questions.explanation: Anyons have exchange statistics beyond the usual bosonic (+1 phase) or fermionic (-1 phase) behavior. When two anyons are exchanged in 2D, the wavefunction can acquire any phase e^{i*theta}, and for non-Abelian anyons, the state can undergo a unitary transformation. This is only possible in 2D systems.

questions.question: In the toric code, what type of excitations are created by applying an X operator to a single qubit?

options.text: Two e particles (vertex violations)
Two m particles (plaquette violations)
One epsilon particle
No excitations are created

questions.explanation: In the toric code, X errors create m particles (magnetic charges) which are plaquette violations - they flip the sign of the plaquette operator B_p. Z errors create e particles (electric charges) which are vertex violations. A single X error creates a pair of m particles on adjacent plaquettes.

questions.question: Why does the surface code provide exponential suppression of logical errors below threshold?

options.text: Because it uses a large number of ancilla qubits
Because logical errors require chains of physical errors spanning the code distance
Because of active error correction at every time step
Because the physical qubits are entangled

questions.explanation: Logical errors in the surface code require error chains that span from one boundary to another (or form non-trivial loops). With code distance d, this requires at least d/2 physical errors occurring in a correlated pattern. Below threshold, the probability of such configurations decreases exponentially with d.

questions.question: What makes Fibonacci anyons suitable for universal quantum computation?

options.text: They have the lowest energy of all anyons
Their braiding operations can approximate any unitary to arbitrary precision
They can be easily created in laboratory conditions
They do not require error correction

questions.explanation: Fibonacci anyons support universal quantum computation because their braiding operations form a dense subset of SU(2). Any single-qubit gate can be approximated to arbitrary precision by a sufficiently long sequence of braids. This property, combined with topological protection, makes them ideal for fault-tolerant QC.

questions.question: The toric code on a torus encodes exactly 2 logical qubits, regardless of the lattice size.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. The toric code has a 4-fold degenerate ground state on a torus (genus 1 surface), which encodes exactly 2 logical qubits. This degeneracy comes from the two non-contractible loops on the torus. The lattice size affects the code distance but not the number of encoded qubits.

questions.question: What is the approximate error threshold for the surface code with minimum-weight perfect matching decoding?
questions.explanation: The surface code has an error threshold of approximately 1% (around 0.8-1.1% depending on the error model and decoder). This means that if physical error rates are below this threshold, increasing the code distance will exponentially suppress logical errors. This relatively high threshold is one reason the surface code is the leading candidate for practical fault-tolerant QC.

questions.question: In a topological phase, the ground state degeneracy depends on what property?

options.text: The temperature of the system
The topology (genus) of the manifold
The total number of particles
The external magnetic field

questions.explanation: A defining feature of topological phases is that the ground state degeneracy depends on the topology of the underlying manifold. For example, the toric code has degeneracy d^{2g} on a genus-g surface. This topological origin makes the degeneracy robust against local perturbations.

questions.question: Which of the following are properties of topological quantum error correction? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Logical errors require non-local error patterns (spanning code distance)
Syndrome measurements are local operations
Error correction works only at absolute zero temperature
An energy gap protects the ground state manifold

questions.explanation: Topological QEC features: (a) logical errors need extended error chains, providing exponential suppression; (b) syndrome measurements are local (vertex and plaquette operators); (d) an energy gap separates the code space from excited states, providing thermal protection. Temperature affects performance but the codes can work at finite T (c is false).
